statement.en: How Britain’s efforts to launder inequality in citizen rights contributed to a forgotten UNGA declaration
statement.summary: This article presents results from new research into United Nations (UN) documents from 1972 to 1985, demonstrating that UK efforts to launder inequality in citizen rights drove the proposal, drafting, and adoption of the 1985 UN General Assembly Declaration on the Human Rights of Individuals who are not Nationals of the Country in which they live (DHRN). The DHRN process began in 1972, as part of a UK response to Idi Amin’s Expulsion of Ugandan Asians, and was adopted without vote in 1985. Today’s six hierarchical categories of UK nationality emerged from efforts over this period to restrict entry to the United Kingdom of some UK citizens, on racial grounds. This article presents an example of how successive UK governments both used citizenship as a tool of international positioning and u

reiTyping.dfumt8Reason: The axiom makes simultaneous claims: (1) historical fact—UK involvement in DHRN drafting is documentable; (2) causal attribution—that this involvement was motivated by laundering inequality, which requires interpretive judgment of intent and causality across actors/decades. Archive evidence (supports) vs. competing explanations of policy motivation (refutes univocal reading). The piece itself is historiographical argument, not settled fact.

ramanujanFormula.interpretation: 権利を国籍で分割し、不正義を言葉で隠すことで、宣言という仮面が生まれる。
